Title: Before the Coffee Gets Cold
Review:
Really interesting idea and I think the limitations of the time travel really added to and helped elevate the general vibe throughout. I think the 2 chapters in the middle (husband and wife and the sisters) were a highlight.

I did find the first chapter to be the weakest and didn't really like that Fumiko didn't have a greater realisation and instead got wrapped up in travelling back in time to prevent a break up. It nearly bugged me enough to stop reading and cast  bit of a shadow over the rest of the book 

I do however think this book did a good job of exploring all that it could with the rules that exist kn the story and think its a nice little one off for a somewhat lazier/easier read

Title: The House in the Cerulean Sea
The Final Empire
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, hopeful, inspiring, mysterious

Title: Vicious
Review: Went into this book fairly blind and found it to be a very fun take on the superhero genre. Excited to see what happens next

Title: The Fragile Threads of Power
Review:
A fun return to the setting and great to revisit the characters and see new ones too. 

I particularly liked Tes and how she could interact with the magic system <spoiler>i think she was a good sort of mcguffin to "fix" kell too but i do think Kell shoildve maybe stayed broken in skme regard as it sort of undercuts the conclusion of the last book

In the same vein i do think the ghost Kosika sees (whether it be Holland or a fragment of Osaron) also kind of undercuts the ending of a conuring of light</spoiler>

Nevertheless I think this was well written and continues to build upon the characters  and their dynamics in fun and interesting ways

Title: The Priory of the Orange Tree
Kiki's Delivery Service
Review: Very wholesome and charming story with incredibly cozy vibes. Jiji is a mood

Title: Before They Are Hanged
Review:
I think this was mostly a solid sequel to the first book and felt that the writing style has improved too. 

I really enjoyed following Glokta's story and honestly find it really funny that the entirety of the inquisition are just sassy mean girls

Jezals character arc was great though this book did not seem to explore why Bayaz chose him and it feels a touch frustrating to only have theories and no answers to questions ive had since the last boom(I suspect he is a bastard heir to the throne and Kingsblood is necessary for Bayaz's plan) 

Interested to see what the final book of this trilogy has in store

Title: The Hate U Give
Review:
Heartbreaking and horrifying at the same time.

Found myself to be on the metaphorical edge of my seat reading this at times.

I really liked that it did not shy away from different aspects of the story (such as the drug-dealing side) and it really helped flesh out the characters

I thought Starrs fears,interests and thoughts really fleshed her out as a strong character that helped the story shine more.

I do think the ending was maybe a touch abrupt but that may have been where I was invested

Title: The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue
Review:
Absolutely beautiful story. Thought the story did a fantastic job of show and don't tell with Addie's Curse. The gradual exploration of Addie's 300 years really helped break up the pace of the story and <spoiler>when she changed the terms it didnt feel like at all.</spoiler>

Between this and Bury Our Bones in The Midnight Soil V.E. Schwab seems to really excel at writing immortals

Title: Piranesi
Review:
I thoroughly enjoyed the mystery of this book and would strongly reccomend any readers go into this book as blind as possible.
 <spoiler>i do wish the nature of the house was explored more</spoiler>. 

Aside from the mystery and larger than life descriptions of the halls I also really liked <spoiler>the epligue were Piranesi and Matthew tried to piece together a new life, i am especially glad that the house remained in his life (admiteddly i think leaving more mystery regarding the house helps add to the magic of this moment too</spoiler>

Title: The Restaurant at the End of the Universe
In Defence of Witches: Why Women Are Still on Trial
Wind and Truth
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, hopeful, inspiring, sad

Title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
The Alloy of Law
Moods: adventurous, funny, hopeful, inspiring, lighthearted, mysterious

Title: Absolute Wonder Woman, Vol. 1: The Last Amazon
Review:
Very cool comic with a fun exploration/use of greek mythology. Fantastic art and great introduction.

I really liked that the threefold aspect of Hecate was captured by giving her three arms and that Hades was made of the inverted flame. Both these made me feel as if there were other fun aspects of greek mythology captured that I missed and adds a measure of re-readability too

Title: Hammajang Luck
Review:
I adored the vibes of this book and it really captured a wonderful cyberpunk setting that felt really lived in.

I found the characters to each be distinct and each one felt really fleshed out too.

I listened to the audiobook and really enjoyed the little bits of Hawaiian culture and pidgin that were sprinkled throughout and gave the book itself a really fun feel and character

There was a couple of times that some things felt as if they lacked weight/consequences and a few times characters seemed to be a little dumb <spoiler>i didnt really feel there was much consequence nor payoff for Malia overclocking herself and I did not think it was much of a "twist" that Angel blacklisted  Edie for example</spoiler>

Overall a really fun sci-fi read

Title: The Witch and the Woodcutter
Review:
I think both the preface and afterword were great for setting and reflecting on the tone and themes of the book and really helped bookend the story within.

As for the story itself I thought it acted as a great vehicle for exploring and exposing these parts of history. 

I liked that it wasn't always Beorn playing the dumb one and the author instead used other characters to explore/highlight and delve into things a bit more 

I did find myself thinking there was a measure of irony that Beorns main motivation/drive was his dead wife and child but it ended up narratively fitting in well with when Morrigan challenged him in on this in the final chapter though part of me felt that this came a bit too late into the book

All in all I found this to be a really good debut novel with 2 really strong main characters

Title: Gallant
Review: Thought this was a really fun take on ghosts and found Olivia to be a strong main character but felt like the other characters in the story didn't really have much presence and felt that the story ended almost abruptly

Title: Slaughterhouse-Five
Review:
I think this book combines tragedy with comic relief in a really interesting way. To have 2 such conflicting attitudes sounds like it may make it hard but I think the sort of pendulum to it all helped add impact throughout 

I do wish I had read this quicker, I think the idea of slipping through time would've been felt all the more if I had simply made more time for the book and would reccomend to others to read it in a shorter span of time if able

Lots of ideas sound odd on paper (such as the lack of conventiona  structure and tragedy blended with humorous prose) but I think vonnegut did a great job of pulling it off and it ultimately kept me really invested in the story of Billy, told through a series of snapshots of his life.

<spoiler>I think the highlights for me were either Derby becoming a character or the war happening in reverse</spoiler>

Title: On the Calculation of Volume I
You Died: The Dark Souls Companion
Contributors: Paul Scott Canavan (Illustrator), Angus Dick (Illustrator), Duncan Harris (Contributor), Tom Bissell (Contributor), Andrew P Hind

Title: The Bands of Mourning
Moods: adventurous, emotional, funny, inspiring, lighthearted, mysterious

Title: The Blade Itself
Review:
Though some quirks of the writing felt repetitive ar times (a bit tired of reading of eyes bulging) I really enjoyed this as a primer for the setting and introduction to the characters. 

The main characters each felt distinct in the way the were written that helped give them identity and each of them also have more depths that are explored even in just this first book

I do unfortunately feel the story itself stopped somewhat abruptly and might have benefitted from different structuring towards the end (though ultimately it is hard to assess without reading the whole series)

Title: The Final Strife
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, funny, mysterious, sad
Review:
Although this book opened a bit slowly and raised some questions that were tough to overlook it really picked up the pace and pulled me in towards the back half of the book or so and did a fantastic job of delivering of some of the questions raised early on 

I enjoyed Sylah being in a sort of mentor role and loved the development of her relationship with Anoor throughout the book though felt like it almost happened too late as there was not more time to explore it before the plot moved on

I liked the simplicity of the 4 rune system <spoiler>I think it also acted as a cool hint towards there being a 4th blood colour</spoiler>. So i was a bit disappointed to see <spoiler>that it was vaguely more complex but thats moreso because i thought the simplicity would be more interesting to write around and itd be interesting to see how this is actually developed on in the series</spoiler>

I thoroughly enjoyed all the twists throughout and did not think any of them were out of nowhere <spoiler>the biggest twist in my opinion was the reveal that there was more land but the bit i liked most about this twist was that it came with the fact that the wardens empire was just a tiny island</spoiler> but I am not sure all of the twists gave the same little bit extra 

I think the trials really aided the pace of the book once they started but I did not find them as interesting as the other parts of the plot that were going on (with the exception of the mind trial)

I ultimately really enjoyed  the writing style and the book as a whole and I think the author did a fantastic job of establishing a gripping setting and leaving enough breadcrumbs to pull people into the series too

Title: Careless People: A Story of where I used to work
Six of Crows
Review:
The plot itself felt very basic as far as a heist story goes and I didn't like that it sacrificed having an ending in favour of setting up the next book. 

However I thoroughly enjoyed all the characters. A few felt slower to warm to but it felt more rewarding once I did come around. For me they helped make the book standout a bit more and really kept pulling me back

Title: Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea
The Well of Ascension
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, hopeful, inspiring, mysterious, sad, tense

Title: The Starving Saints
Review:
Starving Saints is a book that does a great job of portraying parts of fae folklore in a horror setting and I found it to be really thrilling to follow the trio as they unravelled the rules that bind such creatures throughout the book. 

The middle - climax part of the book was fantastic and really drew me in as the the horror aspects began to heighten in the book.

I did find the ending somewhat abrupt and the start a bit slow and found that some emotional weight was lost <spoiler>when Voyne just came back and when both Trelia and Phosyne also came back from the brink</spoiler> 
 
<spoiler>I would've also liked to have a bit more of an understanding/exploration of Phosynes familiars as they ultimately felt like a bit of a narrative cop-out for them to be useful but only as the plot demanded </spoiler>

Title: Storm Front
Review:
I found the urban fantasy setting to be interesting and well described

The plot is a fairly fun vehicle for exploring the setting and the magic system itself had enough draw to keep me invested in the story.

As for Harry Dresden, I did not really like him. This was made worse by the fact he was the sole POV and the book was written in first person

Although funny on occasion I generally found him smarmy and chauvinistic. As this was addressed in the book itself I expected Dresden to maybe improve over the course of the book but he did not.

Generally Dresden didn't really feel like he had a character arc, he was simply the person the plot happened to which may be fine in another context but where i did not like him it sort of hung over the book

Title: Shadows of Self
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, funny, mysterious, sad, tense

Title: The Way of Kings, Part Two
The Fall of the House of Usher and Other Writings
Shadows for Silence in the Forests of Hell
Vengeful
Review: Great sequel, loved the gradual ramping up as the characters stories all converged. Really evoked a great comic-booky feel to everything too

Title: A Conjuring of Light
Moods: adventurous, dark, funny, hopeful, sad, tense
Review:
I loved this book as a conclusion to what has been set up over the 2 previous books. I adored the characters growth and development 

VE Schwab continued to deliver on making the costs of magic feel really important <spoiler>i was howver a bit disappointed that we did not get to see the consequences on Tieren of being the anchor for the sleep spell after they addressed itd be costly for someone to do so

I enjoyed Kell rejecting his past and accepting the family he has now and it really twisted the knife when both Maxim and Emira died without being able to reconcile with Kell</spoiler>

Title: Night Watch
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, hopeful, inspiring, reflective
Review:
This was a re-read after maybe 6 or 7 years but is nevertheless still an incredible book.

It is darker than a typical discworld book but manages to keep that same charming pratchett identity to it of which I am so fond.
 
The conflict of Vimes fighting between duty to the people and wanting to get back home to his wife does a great job of showing his drive and devotion and would be a great case study for character.

I think if I had to pick a gripe it would be that the nature of the story meant that the cast was not nearly as diverse as some of the other watch books

Title: How to Kill a Witch: A Guide for the Patriarchy
The Isle in the Silver Sea
Review:
This book has a really interesting premise and magic system that feels perfect for the medium of a book. I loved the idea of tale bound magic. I do wish that the nature of lost tales was explored a bit more <spoiler>like that one epigraph of a man who'd started to forget his wife</spoiler>

The main cast of characters felt really fun and interesting though I feel the villain was a little lacking and had a tendency to sort of act as exposition for the reader 

<spoiler>I expected the book to explore the sort of stealing elsewhere folk to fuel the tales of the isle but it was sort of mentioned and I did not feel there was a super strong pay-off for it</spoiler>

I adored the use of the epigraphs for world building (though admiteddly I like it when any book does this) and <spoiler>I loved the sort of meta-storytelling of archivists losing control with the epigraphs no longer having rhe rulings in part 2</spoiler>

Overall a good stand alone book that did a good job of exploring its world through the vehicle of a charming story that left me wanting to spend a bit more time in the world

Title: Bury Our Bones in the Midnight Soil
Review:
I thoroughly enjoyed this book. Each of the 3 main characters felt distinct in their narrative voices and the similarities that were echoed through each of their experiences helped provide a strong structure and shape to the story. These similarities helped accentuate the difference between each of them too and allowed each character to really shine.

I really enjoyed this take on Vampires and liked how the different rules were explored <spoiler>i especially like the grave dirt charm acted as a sort of Chekovs gun and thought it was a fantastic pay off as it did not feel too obvious but made total sense</spoiler>

<spoiler>If I had to pick a complaint I think the Lottie chapters felt far too dense and impacted the pacing oddly but these ultimately ended up being a fantastic way to paint just how much  Sabine was spiralling out of control and ultimately helped cement Sabine as a villain for the final act of the story </spoiler>

Title: The Very Secret Society of Irregular Witches
Circe
Review:
<spoiler>i liked the bit where odyessyus died in agony</spoiler>

An all round really well written book. I do think Odyessyus appearing slowed the flow a little bit (though not enough to impact my rating). 

This felt like a great sort of pseudo sequel to song of achilles.

Similar to song of achilles alot of the language used seems to echo some translations of The Odyessy (at least the translation i read) which really strengthened the sort of feel/vibe of the book

Title: So Long, and Thanks for All the Fish
A Darker Shade of Magic
Review:
Fantastic world(s)building throughout this book. 

Did feel as if something was missing both plot and character wise. I think this is mostly due to the small cast of characters and the fact that some plot points that were alluded to never felt explored enough (such as Red London abandoning White London)

In any case I am excited to see what is next in this series

Title: A Gathering of Shadows
Review:
Fantastic sequel. I do wish we got to spend a bit more time going to other London's but realistically I think the book benefitted a lot from focusing on Red London and building both characters and world there. 

<spoiler>Exploring the nature of Rhys resurrection was great too and added a really good feeling of an "appropiate cost" to the fact he was resurrected in book one</spoiler>

Title: Legends & Lattes
Review: Very quaint and cozy little tale. Incredibly endearing cast of characters and a nice simple and charming premise

Title: Rhythm of War
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, hopeful, inspiring, mysterious, sad

Title: Black Sun
Review:
Fantastic introduction to an exciting fantasy world. I liked the characters though felt as if Okoa was maybe a touch under utilised.

Would have liked to have more of a concrete conclusion but am excited to read the next book regardless

Title: Frankenstein
Contributors: Maurice Hindle (Contributor), John William Polidori (Contributor), Lord Byron (Contributor)

Title: The Ballad of Songbirds and Snakes
Warbreaker
Moods: adventurous, dark, hopeful, inspiring, mysterious, tense

Title: Accessing the Future: A Disability-Themed Anthology of Speculative Fiction
The Song of Achilles
Review:
Heartbreaking story. The writing style managed to capture a very similar vibe to that of The Odyssey (whilst also managing to be far more interestingly written). 

Patroclus made for a great POV character and I really enjoyed following him throughout the narrative, <spoiler>especially after his death</spoiler>

Title: You Weren't Meant to Be Human
The Way of Kings, Part One
The Three-Body Problem
Review:
Wonderful piece of sci-fi writing. The trisolarans and their technology is a great example of just how alien aliens could feel <spoiler>admiteddly this is somewhat aided by the fact that they were not encountered in this book which helped build up their mystery</spoiler>

The sciencecy bits did feel a little dry at times but were nevertheless interesting

However I found the dialogue to feel a little stilted and unnatural at times which took me a bit out do the story (though after the first half of the book or so they did start to feel a bit more normal and familiar)

Title: The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue
The Lost Metal
Moods: adventurous, emotional, funny, hopeful, lighthearted, mysterious, sad, tense

Title: The Will of the Many
Review:
i enjoyed reading this book: i found the magic system to be pretty unique, the main character pretty likeable, and i enjoy political intrigue. i just wish this book had a bit more of an overall impact for me. i wish i had felt a bit more connected to it, but unfortunately, although i enjoyed the individual elements of the book, the overall thing just felt a bit generic.

i’m hoping that the more complex topics get touched on next book, and i absolutely plan on reading the next one.

the character development didn’t feel strong, there was clearly a lot of misogyny in this world that got very brushed aside, and i am not really a fan of ‘the chosen one’ trope.

Title: Read Dangerously: The Subversive Power of Literature in Troubled Times
The Assassin's Blade
Review: i didn’t like this as much as the others in the series

Title: Thistlefoot
Review:
the second half of this book was jaw-dropping-ly good.

i had some issues with the start, where it is unclear when this book is set - they say someone doesn’t have a smart phone but then also don’t appear to search for anything online when looking for information. and it’s unclear if magic already exists in this world. it meant that every now and then i got taken out of the story by throw-away sentences. i worry the beginning almost took itself too seriously and didn’t lean into the folky vibe enough, almost like it needed to be more camp.

however, the second half was perfection.

this book is so beautifully poetic. it reads like a folk tale. there is such beautiful integration of eastern european folklore with real events.

Title: Tomorrow, and Tomorrow, and Tomorrow
Review: too cringey for me

Title: He/She/They: How We Talk About Gender and Why It Matters
Every Day I Read: 53 Ways to Get Closer to Books
Gold
Review: the last book was a struggle… this one was unreadable

Title: Conversations with People Who Hate Me: 12 Things I Learned from Talking to Internet Strangers
Review:
this book was a positive reflection on how to communicate with those we disagree with

i think it did give me some useful information on how to approach conversations in the future and i think he had a good way of discussing the different pitfalls difficult conversations can have

overall i do not think this book was revolutionary though, i think this book could be summed up by ‘treat others how you wish to be treated’ which i think is how we should always act, regardless of being on the internet

Title: A Court of Wings and Ruin
One Dark Window
Review: i loved this book so much, i think my only criticism is that i would’ve loved for there to be even more build up between FMC and MMC

Title: A Court of Thorns and Roses
A Court of Silver Flames - Azriel Bonus Chapter
Emma
Review:
overall i really enjoyed, just not quite as much as pride and prejudice or northanger abbbey.

definitely got confused during some early chapters, but i think it does normally take me a bit to adapt to the older writing, and i just found this one took an extra minute 😅

emma really said “you can either be broke or single, not both” and she is choosing single

mr woodhouse flipped between being relatable and endearing, and so fucking annoying. especially when his main goals were to prevent his daughters from being happy.

i surprisingly enjoyed some of the miscommunications, i found them endearing, unlike when i normally find miscommunications frustrating. i think it does a good job at showing social dynamics and misinterpreting social cues.

i was a bit confused <spoiler>that they judged people for having a secret engagement but then kept their engagement a secret??</spoiler>

Title: Vicious Fae
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, funny, mysterious, sad, tense
Review:
i will start of with my two negatives:
  • i was so excited for female friendship to play a part in this one, i thought that’s what titan was foreshadowing and with laini inviting elise on a night out… then all we got was “i’d had a brilliant night out with laini”
  • i still think there’s a serious lack of diversity

that being said this book made me feel EVERY emotion

i cried. a lot.

and i am so happy and sad at the ending!

as someone who wasn’t that into this series before this book, this book was a game changer

my small prediction for the future: <spoiler>i think titan is dodgy</spoiler>

Title: The Log Books: Voices of Queer Britain and the Helpline that Listened
Heavenly Tyrant
Review:
i don’t think this was as good as the first book but was still an interesting read.

i think where this book fell down was treating readers like they’re stupid and spelling everything out. meanwhile Zetian is being the dumbest bitch alive.

similar to the first one, in such that the feminism, socialism, etc., are very basic and for the most part really obvious.

trigger warning is definitely needed as there is some scenes were consent is dubious and all together feels pretty gross. 

there was one plot twist that i definitely didn’t see coming, so i really enjoyed that.

overall, i still wanna read the next one.

Title: Warrior Fae
Review:
this book started good but the whole second half just felt like a bit of a let down.

the end, where they keep flashing forward again and again started to irk me, the one where <spoiler>gabriel got the phone call from orion</spoiler> should’ve been the end. the way <spoiler>king died</spoiler> was not the worst part, only because it got overshadowed by elise somehow <spoiler>ripped the veil between life and death?!?! like stfu, that felt out of nowhere</spoiler>.

the biggest positives of this book was the interactions between the main 5, which continued to be so loveable.

Title: How Far the Light Reaches: A Life in Ten Sea Creatures
Maid: Hard Work, Low Pay, and a Mother's Will to Survive
Disability Visibility: First-Person Stories from the Twenty-First Century
Crown of Midnight
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, mysterious, sad, tense
Review: this book had me shouting, crying, gasping, i’m still traumatised

Title: The Ashes and the Star-Cursed King
Review: [edit] did a reread before the fallen and the kiss of dusk and i’d probably rate it a 4.75 now, it’s even better on a reread

Title: Tourist Season
Review:
let me preface this by saying brynne weaver will always be queen and an autobuy, buttttt i feel like the last 100ish pages of her books are a bit repetitive, just hitting similar story points.

however, i do love the setting, i have fallen a bit in love with cape carnage, and i still really enjoyed this book.

i have decided that where i draw the line is <spoiler>nipple piercings during sex haha</spoiler>

Title: Assistant to the Villain
Review:
warning: the narration on the audiobook is not good

i’m not gonna lie and say the writing was the best i’ve ever read, but the vibes were so good!

it’s cliche in places yes, but it’s also makes fun of cliches in other places.

i love a down-bad, bad boy and the yearning is on point.

Title: Six Scorched Roses
Review: i’m not normally much of a novella reader but i really enjoyed this one. i think some things felt a little rushed but that’s pretty standard for a short book

Title: Off with Her Head: Three Thousand Years of Demonizing Women in Power
Leaving Isn't the Hardest Thing
Review: love the raw and unfiltered perspective

Title: Gleam
Review:
this series really picks up in this book i feel, im actually hyped to carry on this series now

i will admit the spicy scenes weren’t it for me, didn’t love a lot of the dialogue but i can live with that

Title: Mother of Death and Dawn
Moods: adventurous, challenging, emotional, hopeful, inspiring, sad, tense
Review: i already miss max

Title: Cursed Fates
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, mysterious, sad, tense
Review:
this series is definitely getting better as it goes along, the writing improves for sure!

i do take issues with the representation of bi/pan people in the books and it really fucking bugs me how they talk about mildred. you’re meant to be 27 and talking about how gross a girl is because she’s muscular and talking about her ‘mole-ridden’ skin and body hair??? like stfu. darius can not want to marry her but can we not make it about her appearance and natural bodily things.

Title: Ruthless Fae
Review:
i liked this one quite a bit more than the first, it definitely feels like the first and second were almost 1 book, the way that the scene is now set out

there was definitely still some cringeyness and some repetitive lines, but overall i thought the writing was better 

definitely getting invested

Title: A Billion Years: My Escape From a Life in the Highest Ranks of Scientology
The Songbird & the Heart of Stone
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, mysterious, sad, tense
Review:
i love mische so i was so ready for this book and i have now fallen in love with asar

gave me hades and persephone vibes and i would die for luce

Title: Sorrow and Starlight
Review:
i didn’t tandem read this but maybe i should’ve because this book was so long and boring.

this book added a lot of new storylines: <spoiler>the star with calydinius, the people still at ZA, and soooo many new POVs?!</spoiler> i wonder if this was because the authors didn’t know how to wrap it up or wanted to do more books. like what the fuck was the whole situation with <spoiler>the oracles/witches things, it was weird af and again just opening weird new storylines. </spoiler>

i already felt like this world had no consequences and this book reminded me that no matter how much things look fucked, they’ll be undone by the end of the next book.

these books aren’t known for consistency but the number of plot holes that popped up in this one was a new level for me like i don’t get why dante and leon couldn’t find gabriel, they have a tracking bond thing?

half way through i felt like id read a whole book but one where nothing happens.

also at this point, <spoiler>caleb and seth’s relationship isn’t a slowburn, it’s just an endless cycle of never getting together, like have i been wanting this for 4? 5? books.</spoiler>

Title: I'm That Girl
Moods: emotional, funny, informative, inspiring, reflective, sad
Review:
jordan chiles really is that girl.

this book is so well done. i think it reflects a lot of people’s experiences in elite sports, whilst showing her personality through it all.

Title: Blood of Hercules
Contributors: Meg Sylvan (Narrator), Aiden Snow (Narrator), Teddy Hamilton (Narrator), Tristan Morris (Narrator)
Review:
it’s definitely cringey in places, her narrative can feel quite young (which is expected, she is a traumatised child), so i’m glad <spoiler>nothing romantic happens until she has mentally developed.</spoiler>

i think they did a really good job at <spoiler>making the crucible feel like hell, </spoiler>and actually feel difficult. i think so often it’s hard to realise how hard the trials, etc., are in fantasy books.

there was a fair amount of repetition (like petra being the statue of david and ‘time warped’).

i think augustus was meant to be valiant, but it came across real misogynistic.

the ending had me though and i’m so excited for book 2.

Title: Groomed
Review:
this is a heartbreaking memoir

it draws a lot of similarities for me to “know my name” by chanel miller

it is very well-written

Title: Stolen Innocence: My Story of Growing Up in a Polygamous Sect, Becoming a Teenage Bride, and Breaking Free of Warren Jeffs
Beyond Belief: My Secret Life Inside Scientology and My Harrowing Escape
Dark Archives: A Librarian's Investigation Into the Science and History of Books Bound in Human Skin
I Who Have Never Known Men
I Want to Die but I Want to Eat Tteokbokki
Review:
this was short, so i’m looking forward to reading the follow up.

it started to lose me at the end with the endless metaphors, that as Baek Se-hee states, can become empty. i think the end also made me agree with the psychiatrists conclusion that they often think in extremes.

i really enjoyed the psychiatrists note as i did find myself judging some of their counselling. whilst i think some of my judgment probably came from cultural differences, the note was a good reminder that the psychiatrist was a human, who was not re-writing and editing their responses, they were responding in real time and it’s okay if they weren’t 100% perfect.

Title: The Awakening as Told by the Boys
Review:
i’m reading this after i have done ZA 1-4

i felt like this didn’t really add anything

i feel like you don’t need to know every single persons POV when you already have an idea of who that person is and can understand that they probably had a motive and weren’t just randomly evil sometimes

i think i always assumed that these were characters that had their own reasonings and own stories and so i didn’t feel the need to have that spelled out in this book?

strongly agree with the review that said it reads like fanfiction

Title: Invisible Women: Exposing Data Bias in a World Designed for Men
Review:
this book was so interesting!

i do wish i’d done it as a physical book rather than audio because some of the figures were hard to get my head around without seeing them and being able to compare easily. it did sometimes feel like numbers were being thrown at me, i think the audiobook could’ve benefited from an accompanying PDF for graphs, etc.

a lot of statistics, especially in the UK/US showed the biggest impacts were to bipoc people, and whilst this was acknowledged, this book failed to highlight that the gender gap affects bipoc more prominently because of systematic racism.
also some of the language used erases the experience of trans women and trans men. especially as period products/pregnancy were framed as only ‘women’ issues. 

there were multiple times this book left me speechless.

this should be seen as iconic feminist literature.

Title: Why on Earth: An Alien Invasion Anthology
Contributors: Emily Lloyd-Jones, Laura Pohl, Rebecca Kim Wells, Eric Smith, Alex Brown, Beth Revis, Julian Winters, Maya Gittelman, S.J. Whitby, M.K. England

Title: How to Kill a Witch: A Guide for the Patriarchy
Review:
this felt like a natural follow up to ‘in defence of witches’.

this did a really good job of discussing the gendered issues with the witch trials (although it did use sex in place of gender, a couple of times). i had not heard much about the scottish witch trials in particular so i particularly enjoyed hearing the stories from scotland.

i felt like i was learning something new every other page, even as someone who has always been interested in this topic.

Title: The Wrath of the Fallen
Review:
it’s been a while since i read the first 3 books in this series but i remembered enjoying them a lot and so was really looking forward to this one. i even read loads of summaries of the previous books to make sure i was up to date, but despite that i still felt pretty lost at the start of this book. like i said though, that may just be from the time between books.

overall this book felt a bit all over the place. the pacing felt really messy. some things seemed to take forever, whilst others happened instantaneously. i especially didn’t like that it felt like a lot of information was just revealed during monologues (<spoiler>like i feel like they brought Unir back from the dead just so he could monologue</spoiler>). i also got bored of the characters repeatedly <spoiler>nearly dying and always finding a way to come back.</spoiler> it meant the book had no stakes for me and i felt detached emotionally from the plot.

the only characters that had any growth were miska, <spoiler>isaiah and kaden.</spoiler> all of whom where the most interesting characters by far. so i got bored every time i had to go back to samkiel or dianna, who felt stale and their relationship no longer felt empowering but rather codependent and toxic. the only scene where samkiel had any growth was during his conversation <spoiler>with kaden, where they address kaden’s feelings for dianna,</spoiler> but then during kaden’s pov later he diminishes his entire growth and actions during that conversation.

on a personal note, i also just feel like the plot lines and characters i enjoyed previously were ignored in this book.

some things that didn’t sit right with me:
  • the random nismera sex scene - this felt forced and icky
  • i couldn’t keep a word count for how many times ‘bitch’ was used, but it was always thrown at women and as far i’m aware these characters aren’t meant to be misogynistic
  • there are loads of bi/pan characters but we seem to only be getting any development and focus on MC relationships, in my memory i can’t remember any same-sex sex scenes but this is supposedly a queer normative world?

Title: This Is Where the Serpent Lives
Every Drop Is a Man's Nightmare
It's Not That Radical: Climate Action to Transform Our World
Review:
this is a really important book. it is a super informative and accessible introduction to climate justice activism.

this book is incredibly good at being hopeful and optimistic about a topic so easily bleak and daunting.

my only criticism would be that i think in places it was overly optimistic (although better to be overly than underly) and i do think it would be a good jumping off point for someone who does not already hold similar beliefs. this book is definitely for those who already hold these beliefs.

Title: Role Model
Review:
i think the MVPs of this series are:
  1. ilya - been obsessed with him from the very first book
  2. wyatt - the sweetest little pumpkin pie of a man

i don’t like how long it takes troy to reach out to <spoiler>ryan price. i think it’s nice that he does it in person, but ryan deserved one sooner - not just because troy had a near death experience.</spoiler>

it feels like a bit repetitive that in every book, after the first time they get together<spoiler> they always say something along the lines of ‘it was only a hook up’, ‘it didn’t mean anything’, ‘it was just convenient’, etc.</spoiler>

i smiled a lot at this book, which is annoying because i think troy was a real dick to harris at the start and i don’t wanna forgive him. i think with it being such a short book, i found it hard to really believe troy’s growth. the end was so sweet though that i did cry!

Title: The Big A.S.S. Party
Review: i really enjoyed this little story! i haven’t really liked the other interludes (or whatever you wanna call them) so far but this one warmed by little heart, i love max and geraldine!

Title: The Cult of Trump: A Leading Cult Expert Explains How the President Uses Mind Control
Defy Me
Contributors: James Fouhey (Narrator), Vikas Adam (Narrator), Kate Simses (Narrator)
Review:
i’m so sad that i feel like this series has gone downhill

i’m sure there were some contradictory points (<spoiler>like warner saying he’d only ever had 1 panic attack in the last book then this time talking about ‘the panic attacks’</spoiler>) in this book, mostly just throw away lines but it takes me out of it

also don’t get me started in the whole <spoiler>proposal… like you’re 19, you’ve barely met your partner, don’t know what kind of proposal they want or if they’d even say yes, but you can’t wait a week to do it?!</spoiler> ugh i know this is meant to be a YA romance and not necessarily realistic but this was a BIG ICK

some of the <spoiler>memory recovery stuff</spoiler> was interesting and i think emmalines storyline was interesting but those are the only positives i can come up with

Review:

i loved the prose of blue’s language and thoughts.
i loved the way they loved each other and the way they told eachother that they loved each other.
i loved the way the characters showed vulnerability.
i loved how much character development there was in such a short book.
i loved that it had me so emotionally invested.

this almost felt like a split-fiction-esque love story where they are both in such different worlds.

i think i would like <spoiler>the whole of the “letter to kill blue” </spoiler>burned into my heart.

Review:

Title: The Throne of Broken Gods
Review: <spoiler>went from no sex at all to a bit of overkill, it’s like they’d forgotten it was supposed to be spicy so they threw in random sex scenes. other than that it was enjoyable and a good book. i knew it hadn’t fully gripped me when i didn’t cry at the end.</spoiler>

Title: Phantasma
Review:
this is definitely a ‘brain off’ book, like there is some fun gothic vibes but would i say this book is well written? no.

i did like ophelia’s character progression and i love genevieve. then the ending was so boring and so predictable. everytime i thought there was gonna be a fun twist to this book, there was not. the whole thing with phantasma is never trust devils and yet none of them really even attempted to trick ophelia at any point and apparently genevieve had an easy af time 😅  rather than ophelia discovering any information in cool ways, she is just told everything, i’m not sure there’s anything she actually figures out for herself. i think i will need to add ophelia to my list of FMC dumb bitches (if you’ve read everflame you know diem’s on there), i don’t hate them, i just wanna scream at them for being so oblivious.

i wouldn’t say this book was bad because the vibes were there but this book was just so poorly executed.

Title: Her Majesty's Royal Coven
Review:
i liked:
  • the matriarchal, flipped-misogyny with the witches and warlocks. it shows how ridiculous misogyny is.
  • theo’s character.

i didn’t like:
  • the references to the witch trials as being real witches who got caught. feels like it undercuts the actual misogyny of the witch trials.
  • it felt like someone took a thesaurus to it, some of the words feel jarringly out of place in a conversation.
  • how comically evil the bad guys were, there was no nuance or commentary.
  • some of the people that i don’t think were meant to be ‘bad’ were so unbearable and it felt like they never developed? i couldn’t tell why they were made out to be the ‘heroes’ when they were all very hypocritical and unwelcoming.
  • i think it did a good job at showing transphobia within the LGBTQIA+ community but i honestly don’t even know if it was trying to. 
  • the ending was shit.

it all just fell really flat for me. there wasn’t enough plot, and no real conflict.

(i also found a lot of editing errors, this isn’t something that would effect my rating but it does take you out of it)

Title: We Refuse: A Forceful History of Black Resistance
Brown Album: Essays on Exile and Identity
The Serpent and the Wolf
Review:
Vaasa and Reid reignited my love for romantasy.

“she never told him that she would stay” broke me 😭

if you like the bridge kingdom series but also a bit of daughter of no worlds, this is for you.

i did have ONE problem. <spoiler>I am so sick of the “you think they’re dead, or are they are dead, but don’t worry magic brings them back”, i have read it so many times that i no longer care when anyone dies because i know they’ll come back. i found myself just not caring at all when Reid died because i predicted that she’s just make a bargain. Which was kinda sad as I was so invested in these characters that i wanted to go on a rollercoaster of emotions.</spoiler>

Review:
i’m glad i read this after dark harmony because i don’t think i would’ve enjoyed dark harmony if i had. this book is well written and i liked hearing des’ story prior to meet callie, but i had problems with the rest of the content.

i got the ick when he realised humans had value because one hot woman was kind of smart when talking to him for like 5 minutes??

THEN the rest of the book was him lusting over a child?? no thank you. how did this feel so much less noncy from her POV than his?? 

and when he basically says he didn’t really care about how the sleeping women in the coffins came to have babies until it was callie that was taken?? this man has apparently never heard of empathy.

this whole thing was one big ick, put me off him entirely, which is sad.

Title: Guards! Guards!
Contributors: Ben Aaronovitch (Narrator), Bill Nighy (Narrator), Peter Serafinowicz (Narrator), Jon Culshaw (Narrator)
Review:
i don’t think it was coincidental that everytime i tried to listen to this book, i ended up going for a nap instead.

i read this for a book club, it is not really something i would normally pick up. i like fantasy so i thought it would be okay, but unfortunately i just could not get into this book. i have not read the rest of discworld, and my opinions may be different if i had, but i was told this was a good place to start the discworld universe.

there were some positives:
  • ‘the curious incident of the orangutang in the nighttime’ was the best joke in it
  • Carrot was endearing
  • the brotherhood (especially their first appearance) reads like a monty python skit
  • the premise really appealed to me
  • <spoiler>Carrot arresting the dragon</spoiler>

don’t even get me started on:
  • <spoiler>the orangutang randomly fucking off into time and space</spoiler>
  • i can not hear another repetitive joke where someone was stupid so misunderstood something 
  • <spoiler>“it’s not a bastard, it’s a bitch” 🙄 i hate everything about that and why does it even matter if a dragon is a woman?! what makes them a woman?! the men could give birth like seahorses</spoiler>
  • the fatphobia

i found the rest of the characters so unlikeable and didn’t really care about anything they did. i think they could’ve got rid of 90% of the characters and you wouldn’t notice, they all seemed to blur into one. they all seemed to have the same conversations over again.

the racism in this book felt unnecessary and without real meaning or commentary (again, this may be different if i had read the rest of discworld). there was a lot of underlying misogyny, there was not a single well-done female character and this book definitely wouldn’t pass the bechdal test - although it was surprisingly trans positive, despite having no LGBTQIA+ characters. overall, the most non-diverse set of characters ever. the politics could be on purpose and play an important part in the bigger series but it just felt unnecessary in this book.
i want to caveat that i know this is an older book so i wasn’t expecting zero misogyny, fatphobia, etc., but i think we shouldn’t just accept everything because ‘it was a different time’.

i found the audiobook hard to follow, where it would flip between scenes and character POVs without any notice, i think this is where chapters are normally important and tbh im not sure why pratchett doesn’t use them - unless he was just trying to be edgy.

overall, i think this writing is not for me. it was lighthearted and kinda funny, so i can see how others would enjoy this, i just think it lacked real content. i reckon pratchett just thought up some good one-liners so tried to make a whole book around them.

Title: The Future Is Disabled: Prophecies, Love Notes and Mourning Songs
Bunny
Review:
i think that this book was an interesting satire, but at times it tried a bit too hard to be artsy and poetic, which took away from the reader experience.

i liked the premise of it being a critique of the centring of men in females work and being about the creative process. i think as someone who is not particularly creative and definitely not a writer, parts just didn’t interest me as much.

i think the mean girls comparisons ive seen feel apt, i almost with this book had gone weirder with it, rather than just going for shock.

overall i thought this book would be weirder. i predicted the ‘twist’ very early on.

also, this book has some weird moments: it uses the slur for romani people a few times, so unnecessarily; and it talks really negatively about medication for mental illnesses.

Review:

Review:

Title: City of Gods and Monsters
Review:
i had a lot of thoughts on this book, and i do want to highlight that it wasn’t alllll bad, like the premise was pretty good but:

  • rule #1: don’t fuck with the dog… honestly almost DNF’d at that point! how was that not listed as a content warning anywhere on audible?!?!?

  • i am normally willing to overlook a bit of an age difference or a young FMC, its normally very easy to hear ‘26’ in your head instead of ‘19’ but loren’s thoughts and actions screamed 15/16 for me. her actions seriously felt young teens, rather than the 19 she was meant to be, and it made the whole relationship aspect incredibly gross and uncomfortable. this was then made worse when she wore a school uniform and was a virgin! it felt very icky and i couldn’t ever bare to listen to the sex scene 🤢

  • there’s also a lot of drunk driving without it ever being addressed??

Title: Octavia's Brood: Science Fiction Stories from Social Justice Movements
Contributors: Tara Betts, Jelani Wilson, Terry Bisson, Mumia Abu-Jamal, Autumn Brown, Tunde Olaniran, Tananarive Due, Dani McClain, Alexis Pauline Gumbs, Gabriel Teodros, Alixa Garcia, LeVar Burton, Kalamu ya Salaam, Mia Mingus, Vagabond, Morrigan Phillips, Bao Phi, Dawolu Jabari Anderson, Walidah Imarisha (Contributor), Leah Lakshmi Piepzna-Samarasinha, David F. Walker, Sheree Renée Thomas (Contributor)

Title: The Starving Saints
Review:
tldr:
  • the vibes are immaculate
  • you fully feel like the characters (confused and horrified)
  • felt very unique 
  • the execution was mildly lacking 

this is a similar vibe to hemlock and silver - though that is the fantasy version of this horror.

this books made me feel dumb cause i swear i never could figure out what’s going on. i feel like im so used to books like this dropping hints on what the solution is actually is, how it’s going to end, but this doesn’t really do that, you are at the mercy of the information the characters have and you are experiencing it as they do, which i think really adds to the tense and confused feelings.

i love an unreliable narrator.

at the start i found that the phosyne and treila weren’t distinct enough to separate between, using the 3rd person pov. the book would use pronouns a lot instead of their names and i would have to go back to figure out who we were talking about. so i found id accidentally merged some of their storyline in my head, which i only figured out later in the book. however, i did do the audiobook so unsure how much that impacted this.

i did enjoy that there were <spoiler>people who wanted to get out - didn’t want to fix everything, even when they felt responsible.</spoiler>

i do wonder whether this book was almost too short to fully get the development of the all the characters - with there being 3 main characters. in parts it felt rushed or i couldn’t quite understand why a character had made a particular decision but i do also think that a lot of the times it  was purposeful, for you to not understand a characters reasonings.

i think these are deeply flawed characters and for this reason i don’t agree with the ending - <spoiler>i think it re-enforced that voyne was correct in their annoyance that they weren’t being ‘properly utilised’ but i think that was just a jealous perspective.</spoiler>

Title: A Strange Hymn

i found this one a bit too predictable and it just didn’t grip me like the first one did

Title: Manufacturing Consent: The Political Economy of the Mass Media
Northanger Abbey
Review:
i really enjoyed this, it was a nice lighthearted read that had me laughing a lot

i hadn’t read any jane austen before so was really happy to like this

i will also resent john thorpe until the day i die

Title: You Might Go to Prison, Even Though You're Innocent
Review:
well-paced, incredibly well written and covered a huge and important issue in a very digestible way.

at times, i wish it went a bit more in depth into the systematic issues as i knew a lot of these things previously, but that might just be as someone who has read/watched media on this topic before.

Title: The Isle in the Silver Sea
Review:
okay so i did honestly have a few complaints, but none of them took away from how much i enjoyed this book overall. this was such a masterpiece overall.

positives:
  • the romance was wow. the yearning, the expression of love, all perfect. and a special shout out to the scene where <spoiler>vina traces all the tattoos she has memorised AHHH</spoiler>.
  • i love that the trans character was not whittled down to just their trans-ness; they were all a whole and complete character that i was so in love with.
  • a lot of the plot felt so unique and didn’t feel like other ‘romantasy’ i have read (i think this would more be a fantasy w/ some romance but idk).
  • the queerness. i love how queer and perfect the whole thing was.
  • the relationship vina has with her father. <spoiler>this had my heart HURTING</spoiler>.

negatives:
  • the start felt too slow for me. i found it focused too much on ‘reading like a tale’ rather than actually setting up the story and making me invested. from half way through though i was HOOKED.
  • i found that a lot of the bigger story was told through like a ‘villain monologue’ <spoiler>where they tell the MC basically everything about what the queen is doing because they think they’ll be dead soon</spoiler>.
  • this is such a small complaint but one of the archivist’s letters <spoiler>a man talks about his wife who disappeared when a tale fell, and it just annoyed me that it never explains why this one man remembers his wife? like was he a one-off? did loads of people have these vague memories because if so, surely people would’ve known it was to do with the tale’s dying??</spoiler>
  • i think the racism ‘us vs them’ side of it could’ve been explored more and elaborated on. it feels kind of just ‘assumed’ <spoiler>that the queen doesn’t want elsewhere people on the island</spoiler> but we never see if this is rampant in everyday life, or how this impacts elsewhere people, who aren’t incarnates.

Title: How to Commit a Postcolonial Murder
Anathema
Review:
would defs recommend everyone check the content warnings before reading. luckily idm a bit of horror but this was definitely graphic.

i struggled to get into the start of the book and it felt like a bit of an info dump.

this book felt like 2 books merged into one. there was this detailed world-building and magic system and really interesting lore, which then felt like it was shoved aside for the romance - this meant that the romance felt forced for me and the rest of the world was brushed over despite being really detailed.

some random thoughts:
  • we love a critique of religion
  • personally am not a fan of the FMC being a virgin, i don’t like feeling like it’s being romanticised 
  • it really did question how men could be the fucking worst, and how they would be even worse with magic
  • the MMC is kinda misogynistic?

Title: Careless People: A story of where I used to work
Review:
this book is rated highly for how interesting it was, not because i necessarily agree sarah’s take

best bits:
  • people letting mark win at catan and i can’t believe it’s a re-occurring theme!
  • the guy having to explain nuremberg 
  • big bird

i knew meta was trying to shut down this book but the irish government should be shutting it down 😅

i thought it was really interesting to see a personal account of stories you hear over the years (and lots that never made it to the news).

this book starts with making you realise how so many people can be antisemitic and fascist so casually. and it ends with just straight up breaking dozens of laws.

so much of this i was disgusted but not surprised. the one that truly got me was the advertising that monitored teen girls emotional states and people who have just had children. it went from ‘classic facebook’ to feeling sick.

sarah does a really good job at making you feel how she does. i truly followed her through blind optimism to the hatred and dread for facebook’s culture.

i think where this really falls down is how she does still believe that this could’ve all gone differently, she doesn’t seem to acknowledge that this is the issue with the system, not just this one company. as is discussed with the taxing and how complicit countries such as ireland were in reducing tariffs for these big organisations, i understand the benefit this has for these countries but it demonstrates that if facebook was the most moral company in the world, they would be expected to turn down benefits that are offered to these big companies.

this book is titled ‘careless people’ and the final chapter is about how they were careless with the power that facebook has but what it does not seem to address is the active steps mark and his team have taken over the years. for any easy example, mark went from marching in pride parades to the anti-LGBTQIA+ makeover more recently. i think naming their behaviour ‘careless’ rather than actively selfish and manipulative downplays their behaviour.

facebook started on misogyny and was therefore always going to be misogynistic (at minimum). i can forgive someone for believing it can be better but i struggled to support sarah as the book went on and she was willing to overlook so many horrible behaviours until they directly impacted her. i think sarah believes she is doing the right thing but i think it’s demonstrated by her moving from working in facebook to AI that she needs to consider how highly she actually values morality.

there was some name-dropping, and as he admits himself he is constantly seeking attention and admiration, i wonder about the benefits of his sharing this diary, or whether it’s again feeding into his destructive cycle

the last 30% of this book is what i think made this book, it was so raw and as someone with depression, incredibly relatable

Title: Unmask Alice: LSD, Satanic Panic, and the Imposter Behind the World's Most Notorious Diaries
Witchcraft for Wayward Girls
Contributors: Hillary Huber (Narrator), Leslie Howard (Narrator), Sara Morsey (Narrator)
Review:
the start was so powerful.

very ‘the craft’ coded.

warning: the audiobook has a lot of wailing.

i actually really liked the start as a sort of historical literary fiction. but maybe that’s just because the thought of pregnancy and childbirth is enough of a horror story. this book almost felt like the meme of the horse drawing where it keeps getting worse (until 2817 weeks, which i actually really enjoyed).

would not recommend to pregnant people or people planning on getting pregnant.

the handling of race and racism felt ‘ignored’ at best. <spoiler>there is one conversation when zinnia arrives, and thats it. not too mention that zinnia quickly forgives rose for playing the ‘white saviour’ role.
hagar being the one to suspect something unnatural is happening feels like it’s playing on ‘the spiritual black witch’.</spoiler> hagar seems to only exist to run around after the white characters, without any real character arc of their own.
however, these are purely my opinions and so i am interested to hear how black people view the portrayal of black characters in this book.

<spoiler>if they can pass the burden on to a child (i know they said they prefer not to) and they’re out of time, why don’t they pass the burden on to the baby in the coven? there’s already a baby in that coven. or to holly’s baby? i get they prefer consent, but they were willing to take away fern’s consent.</spoiler>

<spoiler>it was soooo coincidental that hagar and miriam did exactly what fern and zinnia wanted them to.</spoiler>

i honestly don’t think they were hormonal enough. i have been around pregnant people and there’s is a lot more random crying and anger, both before and after birth. these girls are children. how were they so in-control?

Title: What My Bones Know: A Memoir of Healing from Complex Trauma
Moods: emotional, funny, informative, inspiring, reflective, sad
Review:
this book was so insightful and really took you on foo’s journey in a way i didn’t realise was possible.

i especially loved on the audiobook version that she played clips directly from her therapy sessions.

i do think i would like to separately research some areas, such as the impacts of trauma on the body. i think ‘trauma’ is such a misused term now that it makes it hard to separate supported information. i am not saying that foo misused the term, but more than what we actually know about trauma is often inconclusive and over-stated.

i found her sessions with dr ham amazingly interesting and wish i saw more representation of this kind of therapy.

overall i think so many people who benefit from reading this memoir.

the thing that finally did it in for me was: i feel like this perpetuates the narrative that allegations regarding sexual assault are often false. like this was published in 2022, it should do better.

as a side note, i’m not sure the author has got a tattoo, there was so many wrong things haha.

Title: Pride and Pleasure: The Schuyler Sisters in an Age of Revolution
Review:
i received an arc through net galley

i think this book is slightly different from what i was expecting. so i do not want my DNF to be implied as this being a bad book. the book focused a lot more on the history and context of the time than i was expecting.

the author has been very thorough in gathering information and data from the time (it is very well sourced). in my opinion they just gathered so much information, that the information which was included didn’t feel purposeful, rather like everything that was gathered was included. therefore i found it going on tangents that i couldn’t quite see the reasoning behind, and included a lot of names that i started to get lost with.

as mentioned this was an arc so this may be fixed with further editing.

wow.

c g drews knows how to use metaphors and similes. the writing is so beautiful. the gothic energy was stunningly beautiful and my heart was on a rollercoaster through every emotion.

it gave me a bit of ‘frankenstein’ energy. and i think in the way that frankenstein is about the experience of being a woman, i think hazelthorn is about the experience of being autistic.

Title: The Seven Moons of Maali Almeida
Jane Eyre
Review:
this was going to be a 5 star until that ending!

i looove the way brontë addresses the reader in this.

i felt this book right in the religious trauma. especially <spoiler>when helen dies and she’s scared because she isn’t as strong in her faith as helen.</spoiler>

so let me say about why i HATED that ending. <spoiler>when jane returns to edward, she says he never would’ve actually forced her to stay as his mistress - like bitch he basically threatened to get aggressive if she didn’t agree to run away with him??? like we’re meant to be sooo happy that she got back with this liar? nah.</spoiler> the ending isn’t THE worst because <spoiler>she is now independent, almost has a developed frontal lobe and is now the more powerful one in their relationship. however, i just think her pinning over him stopped her from experiencing more, and when she was younger she wanted to experience more of the world, but she ends up only seeing essentially 3 towns.</spoiler>

my dream ending would’ve been <spoiler>jane travelling to france or germany and running a school for orphan girls, where she makes sure they get a nicer education than she got and she loves all the school girls as if they were her own. in my dream ending she is happy single, childless and being smart and badass.</spoiler>

i love how feminist this book was for the time but i just think it could’ve been mooooore feminist.

as someone who is in their mid-20a with chronic illnesses i found this book an amazing representation of my experiences.

i enjoyed that it was mostly anecdotal (i can see how some would see this is as a negative) but this is an investigative journalism piece, rather than a scientific paper. i thought the author did a good job at representing lots of different peoples experiences and points of views.

whilst i think this book stands out for its anecdotal interviews, i was still shocked (but not surprised) by a lot of the facts and figures in this book.

only thing that marked it down for me was that it did go off on tangents sometimes and could be slightly repetitive in places.

i should’ve DNF’d but i kept wanting to see an improvement, i was rooting for her so much that it broke my heart to realise she was still repeating the same behaviours at the end of the book.

Title: Sociopath
Review:
i think with every memoir that covers childhood you have to have the realistic expectation that some of the details are probably incorrect (this is addressed really well in wayward by alice greczyn). but there are definitely some hard-to-believe stories in this. in particular, none of the internal narrative from her childhood stories felt accurate to a child, but rather a dramatised version of a child’s thoughts, upon reflection by an adult.

there are a lot of inconsistencies, irregardless of age, etc., which makes it hard to believe most of the memoir. so much of this is patric trying to make themselves sound like a genius. there is so many dramatic monologues and many dialogues read like a daytime drama rather than actual conversations or memories.

i am a believer that not all sociopaths are serial killers, etc., and that they can live normal, maybe slightly narcissistic, lives. and i think patric could be a sociopath, but this book did not do a good job of discussing what it is like to live with sociopathy BECAUSE it was written by a sociopath who puts a lot of blame on others for their own behaviour. i think in order to for it to be a good reflection, you need someone who is very self-aware and emotionally intelligent, and i don’t think Patric has that - i don’t know if that is because of sociopathy or not. patric consistently uses their sociopath diagnosis as an excuse for their actions whilst taking no responsibility, and whenever someone tries to get them to take responsibility, they take it as an attack on them being a sociopath, rather that being held accountable.

i have quite a few opinions on the therapist she does discuss, partly because of her description of people with borderline personality disorder and partly because of how she seems manipulated by patric, but i also don’t believe much of the written conversations as they read like scripts so maybe this is all just patric’s interpretation.

this whole things reads like an overly dramatic (but cinematically interesting) movie.

Title: Trauma and Ecstasy: How Psychedelics Made My Life Worth Living
Review:
i received an ARC through netgalley.

alex’s story is incredibly interesting and i think it is fascinating to see this from a first person perspective.

i am not a nuero scientist but i am quite skeptical about repressed memories and how trauma manifests in the body, however i have always believed that a bad mental state can impact your physical health.

i am awed by alex’s open mindedness and willing to explore options outside of traditional treatments, i know i would’ve been right where he started, being pissed off at every doctor and physical therapist that didn’t help.

i think the book would’ve benefitted from including information about how psychedelics can be beneficial, whether there’s any scientific trials demonstrating their benefits, etc., i understand this is not a scientific paper, but i think it would’ve been interesting to see any of the research alex done leading up to or after beginning treatment.

i hope that alex goes on to grow even further and i am happy that his journey has been so beneficial for him.

Title: Unwell Women: A Journey Through Medicine and Myth in a Man-Made World
Review:
as an unwell woman, i loved this.

this went over a lot of the history i had heard, in way more detail. it did a really good job at picking out the important points across a huge amount of time.

i love how much this book credited all the non ‘white men’ that contributed to women’s medicine. i feel like we never hear about the black women who had huge impacts. i am now obsessed with josephine butler.

i do wonder if this book almost tried to cover too much, it was all really interesting but this was essentially a deep dive on ALL women’s health history across the whole globe (although focusing mostly on US and UK). as there was so much, i did struggle to separate a lot of it, in my brain, but that is not a fault of the books, this was just a lot of new information for me.

Title: Perfect Victims and the Politics of Appeal
We Have Reached the End of Our Show
I Never Promised You a Rose Garden
Women Don't Owe You Pretty
Review:
this book is definitely from a point of privilege. but i do think in places Given does a good job of acknowledging that privilege (such as chapter 2).

i find the chapter about calling other people out hard to justify as applicable for those with less privilege, and whilst i agree in principle, i think Given should acknowledge that this isn’t always possible; i would never expect someone to risk their job because they’re challenging their boss and some people are not in secure living situations where they can call out their family, etc.

some comments felt contradictory. Given would say that all beauty treatments or cosmetic surgery are women feeding into the patriarchal/mysogynistic/racist/ableist beauty ideals. then in the next paragraph say that it’s okay to be empowered by different things or that if you’re jealous of someone because of their style, you can go watch those make-up tutorials. it felt like Given missed the line of empowering other women and began judging them for enjoying gender-affirming care.

Given highlights how beauty standards are inherently ableist, which is correct. however, often women with disabilities enjoy beauty treatments such as getting their nails and hair done because they lack bodily autonomy in so many other areas, and when in a body that you are constantly frustrated at, being able to enjoy these things you can control, shouldn’t be shamed.

overall, i had to stop because this book was not encouraging or empowering women. it was judging women for not being as ‘grown’ because of what they enjoy. 

the one area i 100% agreed with Given on is regarding setting standards and acknowledging your worth. i completely agree that women are conditioned to settle or hang on to ‘crumbs’, rather that being confident in their self worth and expecting more from relationships (of all kinds).

Title: Poets Square: A Memoir in Thirty Cats
In Defence of Witches: Why Women Are Still on Trial
Review:
this is a really good, broad approach to how the behaviours that once deemed someone a witch, are looked at today.

i really enjoyed seeing a more euro-centric approach to these topics, as i have mostly read american-centred views.

i will say that i don’t think this book introduces anything brand new or nuanced, but it is an interesting interpretation of existing information and i enjoyed some of the links it made.

i think this isn’t the book for someone who wants an in-depth approach to these topics, as i feel like a book could be written on every other sentence.

one thing i would’ve liked from the book would be about how the traits of those seen as witches, has changed over time, if at all.

edit: i noticed some reviews said that this book was anti-marriage and anti-children, which i personally did not feel this at all - it simply looks at how women are still at disadvantages within these systems.

Review:

the call-backs to juliette’s diary entries was a good idea but poorly executed, i found only a few actually related to the story of this book and that we had heard most of them before! it felt lazy to just repeat diary entries we’d already heard, when it could’ve been much more powerful to have new diary entries that expanded upon what we already knew

i don’t mind that juliette is dumb (she’s young, so of course she’s dumb) but can we please have a bit more development? yes, she’s traumatised, but it doesn’t make a good story if she continues to freak over nothing again and again

the interconnected-ness of everything really makes you feel like you are experiencing all of billy’s life at once, time overlapping, <spoiler>like you are a tralfamadorian.</spoiler>

Title: Poor Little Sick Girls: A love letter to unacceptable women
Review:
this is not quite a memoir, as if focuses on wider issues and uses “we” plural pronouns, but it’s also not quite investigative journalism as it does not include research and references.

when ione talks about going to the nail salon after her diagnosis i really related. i never used to be fussed but as i got older and realised that the reason i couldn’t look after myself the way others managed to was because of a medical condition, i began getting beauty treatments done. i now feel so much better even on those days when i can barely leave bed.

i think at times this came off as judgmental of those who use social media, despite being someone who uses social media themselves. i think many people who discuss self care on social media can be well intentioned, and them discussing issues on social media does not mean that they are not actively doing things to change the system that causes burnout, etc. overall, despite being of a similar age to ione, i think ive had a very different experience of social media, which makes it interesting to hear their point of view, but it does make me struggle when ione makes very conclusive statements about how social media is and how everyone uses it.

the distrust for your own body is so relatable and i really enjoyed hearing someone talk about it.

i think ione glorified past movements (in particular feminist waves) and used them to put down current actions. whilst i do agree with a lot of the critical views ione took of influencers, i do also believe that it is an over-simplification to commend previous movements, whilst not acknowledging many of their faults to an equal extent.

eventhough i agree and relate to a lot of this book, i found the approach to be a bit hostile and conclusive, without having the scientific references to back it up.

Title: You Didn't Hear This from Me: Notes on the Art of Gossip
Review:
a new take on an old topic, with a mix of personal experience, well researched data and alllll the best gossip.

i do worry about the potential fear mongering regarding the ‘surveillance state’. in particular i think it came across critical of those who share stories they heard online, now i do not think this was the intention but i do think it came across a little hypocritical as someone who shared anonymised gossip all the time of their podcast. although i can see where mckinney is criticising those who use names, etc., on apps like tiktok, i do believe that if people really wanted to, they would just as easily be able to identify the people discussed on the normal gossip podcast. i think the book encourages fear over the personal information that can be found online, rather than putting the onus on those who are sharing the stories.

even as someone who did not have a particularly negative opinion of gossip it still made me rethink my own relationship with gossip and how i view its place in the world.

also the bonuses are so fun!

Title: The Bible Says So: What We Get Right (and Wrong) About Scripture's Most Controversial Issues
Review:
i think this is a really interesting and important book for both those who believe in the bible, as well as those who do not.

as someone who was raised in evangelical christianity, this gave a new perspective on some scripture that was ingrained in me from a young age. and whilst i may not necessarily agree with all of dan’s outcomes, to me that feels irrelevant to how fascinating this book is. i think my main take on this is that ‘bible literalists’ are not bible literalists.

i found some chapters slightly more interesting or compelling than others - i really enjoyed the section on homosexuality, whilst i struggled to follow the one on hell and therefore was not as compelled by dan’s explanation. 

i struggled to follow in some places just because this book has so much information on ancient greece and on the historical context that went a bit over my head (as someone who has never been very good at history). however, one thing i think this book did an AMAZING job at, was not just including useless information. dan clearly has so much knowledge of the culture and historical context that influenced the bible, and i think it would be easy for him to go off on tangents about society at the time, but i think dan does a really good job at only including relevant and influential information.

i also never really considered how close in time ancient greece and the bible are, it’s crazy how many references there are to greek mythology in this book.

the ending felt <spoiler>perfectly real. it wasn’t an epic romance that ended with everything being perfect, which i LOVED.</spoiler>

the downside for me was that parts of it felt a bit thrown together and cringey, just small things that made the big plot points feel less thought out. the best parts of this book were in the tiny details, and so sometimes it felt like the big plot didn’t shine the same.

this book really drops off after part 1

i enjoyed part 1 a lot, ‘victory’ and ‘i love girl’ were really funny. then you hit a string of sexist, misogynistic and pretty dull stories. 

i don’t have much to add because some of the other reviews sum it up really well but there is a general objectification of women.

it starts with some stories that i thought were highlighting the issues with some men’s approach to the dating scene, like ‘jen’s street’ but you soon realise that these stories aim to portray women as mean and broken.

Title: The Book of Autumn
Review:
i was fortunate to receive an arc through netgalley. all my opinions are my own.

i love the discussion of christianity and the part it plays in this book - taking real and true aspects of christianity and ancient philosophy makes the world feel complex and complete.

i also loved the setting, i felt like i could picture the places so well and they evoked emotions for me.

i did find that the end wasn’t for for me:
  • i had put all the information together and then had it ‘spoon fed’ to me a little, which made it feel like it was dragging
  • but then i did not feel like any of the conflicts between characters were properly resolved but rather pushed aside, which felt rushed

i think this was a really good story and could just do with some polishing to make the end feel more satisfying.

Title: The Making of Biblical Womanhood: How the Subjugation of Women Became Gospel Truth
Review:
i want to make clear that although i was raised baptist, i no longer am. however, i did want to learn more about why bible teaching is so often patriarchal.

i think this book raises some very interesting talking points and where this book excels is the comparisons to medieval christianity, the author is clearly incredibly knowledgeable on the history of the church.

one area i found lacking was that some parts were displayed as fact, despite being heavily debated, especially when it came to translations (as let’s face it, they will always be debated when it comes to the bible).

my main issue was that you can not truly discuss the role of women within the church without discussing how this differs for trans women and other LGBTQIA+ women. this book seems to exist to support those christians who are already feminists - i do not think it would actually change those who have patriarchal views and it only seems to reflect white cis women’s experiences in evangelical christianity.

i would still probably recommend it to the white christian feminists in my life.
