Title: It Came from the Closet: Queer Reflections on Horror
Half His Age
Review:
i loved how raw and uncomfortable this book was, whilst being poetic and emotive.

i think the thing mccurdy’s writing does so well, is make you feel exactly how you’re meant to. there was explicit sex scenes in this and instead of feeling sexy, it makes you uncomfortable, i had such visceral reactions throughout.

you can see where mccurdy pulled things from her own life (if you’ve read her memoir) but done it in a way where it reminded me so much of how i felt and acted as a teenager.

this definitely needs the trigger warning, but if you like a book that feels uncomfortable, this one is for you.

edit: the longer i sit with this book, i think i would give it a lower rating. i do not find myself thinking about the book in a profound way. i also saw it raised that this book includes the ‘r’ slur which i unfortunately did not pick up on. i will not change the original rating but i think i would likely rate it a 3 now.

Title: Solemates
Review:
i think this was a really good quick look at the history of foot fetishism. i think the writing did a good job at keeping things concise, without hopping from one thing to the other.

for such a short book, i think it was well researched, zmith is clearly very interested and knowledgeable on the subject.

upon reading other’s reviews, they said they wanted a deeper dive on some topics, but as someone who was just interested to hear zmith's perspective, without a deep interest in the topic, i liked that this covered a large breadth of topics without getting boring.

Title: The Final Strife
Review:
my immediate thoughts were how much symbolism there was for real world issues, such as:
  • the reclaiming of a slur
  • mutilation of infants
i picked up on so many throughout.
i particularly like the way they make you feel how important hair is for black girls and how it is used as a representation of themselves.

i love that even in a culture that is so racist, they draw the line at transphobia.

i really enjoy how flawed the characters are. i think the portrayal of addiction was good, with the constant repetition, it made you feel how syla’s thoughts went there again and again. <spoiler>i do think it gets a bit messy when she takes the joba seed again to fight.</spoiler>

I do think some parts were a bit ‘dumbed down’ by repeating points at the start, but there is so much world building that i thinks it’s just so you can take it all in. i thought the ending was going to be predictable, but i was wrong! i actually think it had a really good ending!

i like the subtle shifts towards extremism, it shows how easy it is to begin with similar ideals and be slowly indoctrinated.

whilst i am glad sylah acknowledges her bias and that she has likely contributed to the discrimination of ghostings, i am still unsure why hassa is friends with her? sylah also treats hassa as less than for most of the book.

when sylah starts talking about why more dusters die from joba seed use than embers - wow. such a powerful monologue.

overall, i loved the story, the draws on african mythology and the complex/flawed character development, however the pacing was off, especially at the start, which meant it felt a bit awkward and stiff in places.

Title: The Witch and the Woodcutter
Review:
this linked really well with all my other reading i’m doing atm on the witch trials - i recommend ‘how to kill a witch’ and ‘in defence of witches’.

i really enjoyed the characters in this book, they felt super fleshed out and i got so emotionally invested in them. i also couldn’t stop picturing morrigan as meredith from brave.

the setting was perfect, i could picture everything so clearly and i think that the descriptions were where the writing really excelled.

at the start of this book i was obsessed, couldn’t put it down, but just after the middle it lost me a bit, i think this book went over so many interesting things and made so many interesting references to the past, but i think where it got lost was in the story.

i found the dialogue to be up and down. sometimes it felt like it was trying to get a good quote, which took away from the natural flow of dialogue. i especially felt this <spoiler>when they first entered the north, every line out of morgan’s mouth sounded like it was a quote that could be taken out of the book. </spoiler>this also links a bit to one of my main problems with the final few chapters, <spoiler> because i don’t know what the inquisitors reasoning was for monologising to beorn about the church and power, etc.</spoiler> it felt like everything we had already learnt and explored throughout the book was then being spelt out, like we were dumb.

overall, this is an amazing book about feminine rage, and i love the feminine rage it made me feel but i think the dialogue was stilted and sometimes the book would spell out the obvious, rather than let you infer.

Title: A Short History of Trans Misogyny
Review:
this was incredibly informative and powerful. i think it did a really good job at discussing the relationship between the history of trans misogyny and sex work. i would’ve liked to see more about the history of trans panic, and how that defense has been used/adapted - it felt rushed.

i do think it focused a lot on bipoc trans misogyny history, which whilst incredibly interesting, i would like to see more from around the world. it felt very american and bipoc central.

i think this focus on especially black trans figures, had almost the affect that the book was trying to argue against - the symbolisation of black trans people. in particular, those figures who never identified as trans (or equivalent to trans).

the writing style wasn’t quite for me, just like cadence and sentence style wise. it just meant it took me a little longer to process some of the information.

Title: Broken Fae
Review:
this series is a fun read! it’s not the best writing, but it has definitely improved throughout the series! i also find the characters incredibly loveable and the plot intriguing 

and one of the ‘plot twists’ in this book did actually surprise me! i’d figured out the other stuff, but if this plays out with Gareth then that would be something i wasn’t expecting!

i also want to add the complaint i have with this whole series which is the complete lack of diversity, and i don’t feel like elise is a ‘strong female FMC’, i feel like she has just been given predominantly male traits. i think this series would be so much better with actual strong females, female friendships, proper LGBTQIA+ representation and if it was more culturally diverse, like we get it, they’re tanned white men.

Title: Jesusland: Stories from the Upside Down World of Christian Pop Culture
Review:
i received an arc through net galley.

this book uncovered a lot of memories i didn’t know i had of ‘out there’ christian media while addressing some of the bigger issues christianity plays in popular culture. joelle does an amazing job at blending personal experiences with well-cited research. this book is obviously steeped in personal opinion but i think it is one many ex-evangelicals (and some present evangelicals) can relate to.

how joelle talks about the music in church really resonates with me. as a child, the music was the time i felt most connected to god… i soon realised that this is just how music feels and i could feel that kind of ecstasy at a gig, not just god.

the prologue does state that joelle tries to keep a neutral point of view and not bid for or against christianity and evangelical culture. whilst i do not think this book is entirely neutral, i do not think it has to be. this is ultimately a deconstructed persons point of view and their look back at evangelical culture, and i think it is okay that they took a more critical view, as it shows where their head was at and the things that brought them to their deconstruction. i also found it particularly interesting when it came to the investigating they did as part of this book, where they discover information and context they never had when they were exposed to the media as a child, i think they have a right to be critical of media that was forced upon them as a child. you can also see how joelle’s understanding and relationship with evangelical media changes throughout the course of the book. joelle manages to show respect to the religion without respecting the media.

i feel like it did lose a bit of its voice when discussing the ‘war on terror’; creationism being linked to christian’s being placed in positions of power; and, the climate crisis. i think these veered into bigger topics that would need a whole other book and therefore became less focused on evangelical media. these topics were all incredibly interesting but maybe did not link in as well to the overarching voice of the book. i think this could likely be resolved in editing though, maybe linking back more of deciding what is tangential.

some editing is needed but this was an ARC so therefore that absolutely did not effect my rating.

Title: Will My Cat Eat My Eyeballs? Big Questions from Tiny Mortals About Death
Review:
this is a really interesting little book full of fun facts. i am someone who has always had a lot of ‘morbid’ curiosity so i really enjoyed it. it gave well-founded information in an easily accessible format.

i would’ve preferred for the references to be footnoted or cross-referenced throughout, so it was easy to do further reading on topics, though i note this book is aimed at all ages so maybe that’s why this wasn’t used.

caitlin does use the term ‘developed world’ rather than a term such as ‘global north’, etc.
